segments.text: Welcome back to Tesla Short's Time Daily episode 414. It's March 22nd, 2026, and I'm Patrick and
Vancouver. Let's get into what's moving in the Tesla world today. The big regulatory story
this morning is that NHTSA has officially moved its investigation into Tesla's full self-driving
software up to the engineering analysis phase. This is the stage where the agency starts requesting
detailed technical data, running its own simulations, and really digging into the architecture
of the system rather than just collecting incident reports. For context, this investigation has
been simmering for a while, focused mainly on how the camera-based FSD handles certain crash
scenarios and edge cases. The escalation signals that regulators aren't satisfied with the
explanations they've received so far and want to understand the underlying decision-making logic
at a much deeper level. From a business perspective, this adds another layer of uncertainty
for Tesla as it tries to expand supervised autonomy approvals across different states.
It also puts the spotlight squarely on Tesla's stubborn commitment to a vision-only approach
when many competitors are still hedging with radar, lidar, or other sensors. That said,
it's worth remembering that moving to engineering analysis doesn't automatically mean a recall
or enforcement action is coming. It's more like the agency is rolling up its sleeves for serious
homework. Still, it highlights the challenging regulatory environment Tesla operates in even as
the technology itself keeps making progress. It's interesting to contrast that with what's
happening across the Atlantic because Europe seems to be heading in a more accommodating direction
right now. Tesla has finished its final vehicle testing for full-self driving in the Netherlands and
has submitted all the required paperwork to the Dutch authorities. The regulator there is now
naming for an April 10 decision after having pushed back from an earlier late March target.
This milestone comes after about 18 months of dedicated work to meet Europe's stricter safety
and documentation standards. If approved, it would mark the first supervised launch of FSD
in a major European market, which could open the door for other countries to follow.
According to reports, the testing wrapped up without any major red flags and the documentation
package is now fully in the hands of the regulators. For Tesla, this feels like tangible international
progress on the autonomy front after years of slower movement in Europe compared to North America.
It also shows that while the company faces headwinds with NHTSA, it can still find regulatory pathways
elsewhere when it puts in the sustained effort. That kind of geographic variation in approval timelines
is going to be a recurring theme as supervised autonomy rolls out globally. Of course, all these
regulatory moves only matter if the underlying technology can actually deliver in the real world,
and that's where a recent conversation with a longtime Tesla owner caught my attention.
This person has been using FSD for over six years, which means they've seen pretty much every major
software version since the early days. What they're noticing now is that camera performance in
adverse weather continues to be a meaningful limitation for the vision-only system. Rain, snow,
fog, heavy spray from other vehicles. These conditions still create constraints that force drivers
to take over more often than they'd like. It's surprising, honestly, that this vulnerability has
taken this long to get widespread attention because all weather reliability feels so fundamental to
any serious autonomy claim. Customers who live in places with frequent bad weather think the
Pacific Northwest, the prairies in winter or parts of northern Europe, are already feeling these
limitations in their daily driving routines. This isn't just an inconvenience. It sits right at
the heart of both safety and long-term customer trust. The challenge is baked into the vision-only
philosophy Tesla has bet so heavily on, so it's not something that can be easily fixed by bolting
on another sensor. I think we're going to see Tesla lean even harder on software improvements and
massive amounts of training data from diverse climates to try to close this gap. Additional
real-world miles from places that actually get proper winters and monsoons could help the system
learn those edge cases better over time. And if software gains start to plateau, we might eventually
see some hardware refreshes play a supporting role, though the company has been pretty quiet
about that possibility. While autonomy continues to navigate these technical and regulatory hurdles,
Tesla is still quietly expanding the physical infrastructure that keeps its current fleet happy.
The company has now activated its first true V4 supercharger station on the East Coast,
located in Kissimmee, Florida. This site features eight stalls that are capable of delivering up
to 500 kilowatts of charging power. While individual V4 stalls have shown up in other locations
before, this represents the first full station in the region built around that 500 kilowat
capability. It's also only the fourth such high-power station across the entire United States.
For owners of the latest vehicles that can actually accept that much power, this should translate into
noticeably shorter charging times and less time spent waiting at busy locations. It also sends a
signal that Tesla remains committed to investing in its supercharger network, even as it's opening
those stalls to other automakers. Faster, more reliable charging directly improves the ownership
experience for people who are already in Teslas, and it helps address one of the bigger practical
barriers to broader EV adoption. In a lot of ways, this feels like the unglamorous but essential
work of building out the supporting ecosystem while everyone else focuses on the flashier autonomy
headlines. At the same time, Tesla appears to be laying some early groundwork in markets where it
hasn't even started selling cars yet. The company recently posted a job that points to initial
steps toward entering India's utility-scale energy storage sector with its mega-packed business.
It's worth noting that Tesla still isn't selling vehicles in India, so this energy first
approach is telling. It suggests the company is trying to build a foundation on the grid side
before tackling the complexities of the passenger vehicle market there. This matters for Tesla's
broader international growth strategy because it shows they're willing to think beyond just cars
when entering emerging markets. India has set some very ambitious renewable energy targets over
the coming years, and those goals will require substantial buffering capacity to handle the
intermittency of solar and wind. Megapax could potentially open up a meaningful new revenue stream
if a number of those large renewable projects actually move forward. The beauty of this approach
is that grid-scale storage projects often involve different regulatory pathways than selling consumer
vehicles. They tend to work through utilities and government entities rather than individual buyers.
From a first principles perspective, it makes a lot of sense. Physics tells us that integrating
large amounts of renewable power onto the grid efficiently requires exactly the kind of buffering
capacity that standardized battery systems can provide. Tesla's mega-packed design benefits from
being manufactured at volume in existing factories and installed with relatively little custom
engineering. Starting with energy storage rather than jumping straight into cars may turn out
to be a smarter, lower friction way to build relationships and demonstrate real value in a
market like India. It could reduce some of the political and regulatory friction over time before
the more visible car business enters the picture. China, on the other hand, remains the most
competitive and fastest-moving EV battlefield right now. Xiaomi just unveiled the next-generation
version of its Su7 Electric sedan, complete with upgraded technology and improved safety features.
The updated model comes with a starting price of 219,900 UN, which undercuts the previous pricing
and makes it even more aggressive in the market. Early demand for this new Su7 appears to be strong
based on reports coming out of the region. This move really illustrates how quickly local Chinese
EV makers are able to iterate on both features and pricing. It adds yet another layer of pressure
on Tesla in the world's largest EV market, where the competitive intensity just keeps accelerating.
The combination of meaningful tech improvements being delivered at lower price points makes the
Chinese market particularly tough at the moment. Tesla has to stay sharp on both product and cost
if it wants to maintain its position there. On a more positive note, the commercial vehicle
side of the business is starting to generate some genuinely encouraging feedback from the road.
Early users of the Tesla Semi are praising its comfort, range, and overall performance as the
truck gets closer to mass production. Fleet operators who have had the chance to test it are reporting
clear advantages over traditional diesel semis, especially when it comes to the driver experience.
These positive real world reports help address years of skepticism that surrounded Tesla's heavy
duty vehicle ambitions. For fleets that make decisions based on total cost of ownership,
this kind of feedback could accelerate adoption in the coming years. The comfort features and
consistent range seem to be particularly effective at winning over drivers who spend long hours behind
the wheel. It's the kind of grounded practical validation that matters a lot in the commercial space,
where emotions run lower than they do in the passenger car world. This good news on the semi side
provides a nice counterbalance to some of the regulatory and technical challenges we've been talking
about earlier in the episode. In other regulatory news, NHTSA has closed out a defect petition
that had called for a recall of essentially all Tesla vehicles produced over the past decade
over claims of unintended acceleration. After reviewing the petition, the agency decided it did
not warrant any further action. This closes the book on one long circulating source of potential
widespread recalls that had been hanging around for years in public discussion. The decision
gives Tesla some regulatory relief on an issue that has persisted despite multiple previous
investigations. Interestingly, it comes from the same agency that is currently ramping up its
scrutiny on the FSD software. Taken together, these two NHTSA actions this week paint a mixed but
very active regulatory picture. Some pressure is easing in one area while intensifying in another.
That's fairly typical in this space. The relationship with regulators tends to be complicated and
multifaceted. Before we wrap up, it's worth circling back to that camera and weather limitation we
discussed earlier because I think it's one of the more honest engineering challenges Tesla is facing
right now. A long-term owner with extensive experience points out that the vision only system
still struggles meaningfully when conditions get bad. The fact that this issue has taken years
to surface prominently is telling, especially since reliable operation and all weather feels so
central to safe autonomy. Consistent performance across every condition isn't just a nice
to have. It's essential for both safety outcomes and for building lasting customer trust.
This remains a genuine technical hurdle for the vision first approach. That said, Tesla is well
positioned to tackle it through relentless software iteration and by gathering more training data
from vehicles operating in diverse climates. Every additional mile driven in heavy rain or blowing
snow becomes valuable training material. If software progress eventually plateaus, future hardware
refreshes could still play a supporting role, though the company hasn't signaled any immediate
changes there. Taking a step back from the day-to-day headlines, applying some first principles
thinking to how Tesla's approaching markets like India feels particularly relevant right now.
The popular narrative always focuses narrowly on when the company will finally start selling cars
there, but the recent job posting around megapack expertise shows they're actually coming at
the country through grid infrastructure first. That shift in sequencing is smart. Grid-scale storage
projects travel on different regulatory paths than consumer vehicle sales and tend to involve
utilities and government bodies rather than individual buyers. Starting with energy creates
infrastructure relationships and demonstrates practical value before introducing the more emotionally
charged car business. It could smooth out some of the political and regulatory friction over the
longer term. Keep an eye on how that Dutch FSD decision lands around April 10th and whether it
creates any meaningful momentum for other European markets. These regulatory wins tend to have
a bit of a domino effect when they happen. That's your Tesla news for today. TSLA closed at $367.96
down $14.64 or 3.8%. If you found this useful, a rating or review on Apple podcasts or Spotify
really helps new listeners find the show. You can also find us on X at Tesla Short's Time.
I'm Patrick and Vancouver. Thanks for listening and I'll see you tomorrow.

The primary reason to do this is I unfortunately don't have the time to be consistent with generating
all the content and wanted to focus on creating consistent and regular episodes for all the themes
